resources.string: A public website name should never resolve to a private address like 127.0.0.1 or 192.168.x.x. When it does, that’s a DNS‑rebinding signal — a way for a page to reach devices on your own network. This checks a name on demand; nothing is sent anywhere except the one lookup you ask for.
This public name resolved only to public addresses.
This is a local/intranet name (a literal IP, localhost, or a .local/.lan/.internal/.home.arpa name), so a private address is expected — not a rebinding signal.
No addresses resolved for that name.
Couldn’t resolve that name. Check the spelling and your connection.
Resolves a couple of well‑known public names and confirms they don’t come back as private addresses. If they do, your DNS resolver may be lying — a sign of tampering or a captive network.
Resolver looks honest — the canary names resolved to public addresses.
Warning: a canary name resolved to a private/reserved address. Your resolver may be tampering with answers.
Couldn’t reach the canary names to check. Try again on a working connection.

This audit is rootless and on‑demand — it inspects one lookup when you tap. System‑wide blocking of rebinding answers is available only in the opt‑in Standalone engine, which is off by default and never runs while another VPN is up.
